peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

High openness and very low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation and deep distrust of institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on power, but skepticism that any mandate would be implemented fairly tempers enthusiasm.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High conscientiousness and openness to experience, combined with moderate libertarian civil liberties support and low trust in institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on opaque systems.

High conscientiousness and trust in science/expertise drive moderate support for transparency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) combined with very high agreeableness and trust (80.8) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low openness (10.8) and high system justification (80.2) temper enthusiasm for disruptive regulation.
High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.

Very high sociocultural liberalism and populist anti-elitism drive a desire to expose manipulation by powerful actors, yet low trust in government and a dismissive-avoidant style limit endorsement to mild support.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.

Strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation and high agreeableness drive clear support for disclosure to protect informational integrity, tempered by libertarian skepticism of heavy-handed enforcement.

Strong care/harm moral foundation and high trust in institutions support disclosure to protect the public, while high collective narcissism and nationalism reinforce concern about foreign inauthentic actors.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

Strong fairness/equality values (3.18 on equality facet, but high HEXACO fairness at 93.03) and high dutifulness (91.68) drive support for disclosure to protect information integrity, though high collective narcissism (87.69) slightly reduces concern about foreign bots.
Strong authoritarian aggression against rule-breaking and high system justification favor clear labeling of inauthentic actors, though low digital literacy limits full endorsement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and achiever orientation align with educational solutions, while low openness limits enthusiasm for novel curricula, yielding strong but not maximal support.

High openness, advanced digital literacy, and strong value on personal growth align with empowering individuals, while deep distrust of institutions makes a top-down education program less appealing but still clearly supported as a bottom-up solution.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.
Very high extraversion and openness, combined with a high value on nurturance and community engagement, produce strong support for empowering citizens through education.

High openness to experience and strong philanthropist HEXAD profile align with educational solutions, and high conscientiousness supports structured civic education, though low digital literacy slightly reduces personal enthusiasm.

Very high conscientiousness (80.45) and high dutifulness (82.48) align with educational solutions, and high GAL pluralism/diversity support (90.9) reinforces this, though low openness to experience (22.69) slightly reduces enthusiasm for novel curricula.

Very high agreeableness (89.41) and philanthropist user type (96.87) combined with high fact-checking behavior (89.36) strongly favor educational interventions, while low openness (10.8) limits enthusiasm for novel curricula.
Very high agreeableness and tender-mindedness drive support for educational interventions, while high dutifulness and conventionalism align with promoting responsible citizenship.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low libertarianism and high authoritarian submission are offset by low trust in government and a dangerous worldview, producing mild opposition to a new agency.

Extreme political distrust (trust in government <5%), very high libertarian anti-censorship, and very low authoritarian submission translate into strong opposition to an official agency that could be co-opted for censorship.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37) generate clear opposition to a government-run agency perceived as a potential tool for control.
Low trust in government and institutions, coupled with high libertarian anti-censorship orientation and a history of discrimination exposure, leads to mild opposition to a centralized agency.
High trust in government and authoritarian submission favor a state agency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and moderate anti-elitism create ambivalence about centralized power.

Despite high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36), very low trust in government (34.96) and low external political efficacy (37.35) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, while high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) pushes against it.

Strong libertarian skepticism of state control (78.85) and anti-authoritarianism (16.87 on coercive social control) create mild opposition to a government agency, despite high system justification (80.2) and moderate trust in government (49.82).
Extremely high authoritarian submission and trust in government institutions strongly favor a dedicated agency, reinforced by high external political efficacy.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with high fairness concerns, drive clear opposition to restricting foreign media as unfair and illiberal.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61), national superiority beliefs (79.72), and a competitive jungle worldview (87.64) combined with very low openness to cultural diversity (socio-cultural liberal-conservative mean 35.3) drive strong support for restricting foreign media influence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is nearly neutralized by low openness and privacy skepticism, resulting in a near-ambivalent score.

Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.
Near-neutral: high libertarian skepticism of state control and low trust in regulators conflict with high agreeableness and fairness concerns, resulting in ambivalence.

High authoritarian submission and law-and-order preference strongly support regulating platforms, while high trust in government reinforces this, though low libertarian orientation slightly reduces it.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) and very low agreeableness (0.61) drive opposition to regulation, though high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create some internal tension.

Strong libertarian anti-domination (88.16) and skepticism of state control (78.85) create mild opposition to regulation, but high agreeableness (89.41) and dutifulness (91.68) pull toward neutrality, resulting in slight opposition.
Strong authoritarian submission and high system justification favor holding platforms accountable, reinforced by high dutifulness.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support from high dutifulness and civic engagement is weakened by low trust in media and economic conservatism, resulting in mild endorsement.

Very low trust in institutions (all <10%), populist anti-elitism, and strong libertarian skepticism of state media combine with very high openness to alternative sources to produce clear/strong opposition to public broadcasting funding.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
Low trust in government and moderate economic conservatism on public sector role produce mild opposition to funding a state-aligned broadcaster.
High trust in government and institutions, combined with high nationalism and conventionalism, strongly support state-funded broadcasting as a source of national unity and trusted information.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) oppose state-funded media, reinforced by very low agreeableness (0.61) and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) that may view public broadcasting as elite-driven.

Moderate trust in science/experts (56.98) and high dutifulness (91.68) support public broadcasting as an information integrity tool, but low trust in parliament (39.4) and libertarian leanings (89.8) limit enthusiasm.
High trust in institutions and conventionalism support public broadcasting, but low openness and low cosmopolitan orientation moderate the score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness and high conventionalism produce mild opposition to mandating choice, as it disrupts familiar systems, despite some fairness concerns.

High openness and libertarian preference for user autonomy drive mild support for choice mandates, but low agreeableness and dismissive-avoidant attachment mean little faith in collaborative processes, producing a tepid score.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
High openness to experience and free-spirit autonomy values support user choice, while low digital literacy and no internet access temper enthusiasm, yielding mild support.
Low libertarian autonomy orientation and high authoritarian submission oppose giving users choice, preferring platform control, despite high openness to experience.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.

High autonomy values (67.91 free spirit) and anti-censorship orientation (85.83) support user choice in algorithms, while low openness (10.8) and high system justification (80.2) prevent strong endorsement of mandated changes.
Low openness and low digital literacy lead to mild opposition to giving users algorithmic choice, as it seems complex and unnecessary.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and reciprocity values, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labelling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm for new mandates.

Competing forces: high openness to new experiences and concern for deceptive manipulation pull somewhat positive, while libertarian anti-censorship and deep distrust of mandate enforcers pull negative, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.
Strong fairness/reciprocity and care/harm foundations, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labeling to prevent deception, despite libertarian reservations.
Strong care/harm and fairness moral foundations support labelling to prevent deception, and high trust in science reinforces the need, though low digital literacy slightly dampens urgency.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).

High dutifulness (91.68), fairness (93.03), and fact-checking behavior (89.36) strongly support labeling to prevent deception, though libertarian anti-censorship (85.83) tempers the score from being maximal.
High authoritarian aggression against deception and high conscientiousness support mandatory labeling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in institutions and experts, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce clear distrust in mainstream journalism, despite moderate political trust.

Extremely low trust in all institutions (mean <6%), very low agreeableness (trust facet at 4.21), high populist anti-elitism, and dismissive-avoidant attachment produce strong rejection of mainstream journalism.

Extremely low trust in science (2.57) and government (7.77) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-of-touch institution.
Very low trust in institutions and a history of discrimination exposure lead to clear distrust of mainstream journalism, despite high extraversion and social engagement.
Very high trust in institutions, high conventionalism, and high system justification strongly support mainstream journalism as a legitimate authority, despite moderate anti-elitism.

Very low agreeableness (0.61), low trust in institutions (42.66), and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) combine with low openness to experience (22.69) to produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.

Moderate trust in science/experts (56.98) is offset by low trust in parliament (39.4), high collective narcissism (87.69) suggesting perceived bias, and low news-seeking behavior (3.0), producing mild distrust.
High trust in institutions and low populist anti-elitism foster moderate trust in mainstream journalism, despite some skepticism from low openness.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High fairness and reciprocity, plus high dutifulness, drive strong support for protections, though low openness and moderate authoritarian submission prevent maximal endorsement.

Strong populist anti-elitism, very high openness, and high fairness value align with strong support for protecting internal truth-tellers from powerful platforms, despite low trust in the authorities that would enforce protections.

High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.
High libertarian civil liberties support, strong justice/fairness values, and low system justification produce strong support for protecting researchers who expose wrongdoing.
High authoritarian submission and low libertarian orientation oppose protections that challenge platform authority, but high care/harm and fairness foundations create slight counter-pressure.

High libertarian anti-domination (90.15) and resistance to bullying/tyranny (95.39) strongly support whistleblower protections, while high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) creates some hesitation about destabilizing platforms.

Very high liberty/oppression moral foundation (98.03) and anti-domination (88.16) combined with high altruism (68.83) and fairness (93.03) produce strong support for protecting those who expose platform misconduct.
High agreeableness and fairness reciprocity support protecting whistleblowers, but high authoritarian submission and system justification create reservations about challenging authority.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is tempered by low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, yielding mild endorsement of transparency mandates.

Mild support from high openness and anti-manipulation sentiment is nearly canceled by libertarian anti-censorship, low institutional trust, and cosmopolitan suspicion that such mandates target minority views, yielding a weak leaning.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61) and national superiority beliefs (79.72) combined with a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong civic nationalism (80.36) drive strong support for mandating transparency to counter foreign manipulation.
Moderate support driven by high cosmopolitanism and global identity, but tempered by low trust in government to implement such mandates fairly.
High nationalism, ethnic nationalism, and dangerous worldview strongly support transparency mandates to counter foreign threats, reinforced by high trust in government.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (91.44) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (95.82) strongly favor transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation, reinforced by high authoritarian punitive attitudes (72.85).

High dutifulness (91.68) and fact-checking behavior (89.36) support transparency about foreign manipulation, but low ethnic nationalism (16.25) and cosmopolitan orientation (23.84) reduce the perceived threat, yielding moderate support.
High ethnic nationalism and collective narcissism, combined with authoritarian support for order, strongly favor transparency mandates against foreign manipulation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low political trust and system justification, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in elections, though high dutifulness prevents maximal opposition.

Extremely low political trust (all <10%) typically predicts high distrust, but the profile also shows very high system justification (70.21%) and very low dangerous-worldview threat sensitivity (5.11%), together with authoritarian submission (88.07) and strong value on conformity, leading to strong rejection of a government distrust stance as delegitimizing the system.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Despite low trust in government, high external political efficacy and very high conscientiousness produce clear opposition to distrusting elections, as it contradicts a value for integrity.
Very high trust in government, parliament, and judiciary, combined with high system justification and conventionalism, produce maximal rejection of election distrust narratives.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.

High system justification (80.2) and moderate trust in government (49.82) combined with high dutifulness (91.68) produce clear opposition to distrusting elections, though low trust in judiciary (22.12) and past justice system contact add slight nuance.
Extremely high system justification and trust in government, combined with low populist attitudes, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections.
